Board hire is straightforward from operators along the beachfront, and first-timers will find the calm conditions here much more forgiving than ocean swells elsewhere.

reviews.summary: The Papamoa Beach Stand-Up Paddleboarding adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Glide along the calm waters of Papamoa Beach on a stand-up paddleboard. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Te Puke Quad Bike Adventures
pois.short: Ride quad bikes through kiwifruit country and native bush near Te Puke.
pois.commentary: Quad bike tours near Te Puke take riders through a mix of working farmland, kiwifruit orchards, and native bush tracks. Routes include river crossings, hill climbs, and bush trails with views of the Kaimai Range. No experience is necessary as guides provide full training.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived in Te Puke, the kiwifruit capital of the world, but today you are swapping orchards for off-road adventure. Your quad bike awaits on a working farm at the foot of the Kaimai Range, where the tracks wind through a landscape that shifts from open pasture to dense native bush and back again. Your guide will lead you through river crossings, up muddy hill climbs, and along ridgelines with panoramic views across the Bay of Plenty. No experience is required, the bikes are automatic, and your guide sets the pace. This region produces nearly 80 percent of New Zealand's kiwifruit, and you will ride right through the orchards to see the vines up close. Just be prepared to get muddy, that is half the fun.

reviews.summary: The Te Puke Quad Bike Adventures adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Ride quad bikes through kiwifruit country and native bush near Te Puke. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Ohope Beach Coasteering
pois.short: Scramble, swim, and leap off cliffs along the volcanic coastline at Ohope.
pois.commentary: Coasteering at Ohope involves scrambling along volcanic rocks, swimming through surges, and jumping from cliff platforms into deep water. The volcanic coastline near Whakatane provides natural platforms, channels, and deep pools ideal for this adventure sport.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Ohope, where the volcanic coastline creates a natural adventure playground for coasteering, the sport of scrambling, swimming, and jumping your way along a rocky shore. Your guide will kit you out with a wetsuit and helmet, then lead you along a route that weaves between rocky channels, surge pools, and cliff faces. The volcanic rock here has been eroded into platforms at various heights, providing natural jumping spots from 2 metres up to 8 metres for the truly committed. Between jumps, you will swim through rock channels where the ocean surges around you, scramble up seaweed-slicked rocks, and explore caves and blow holes carved into the headland. Coasteering is relatively new to New Zealand but has been hugely popular in the UK and Europe for years, and the Bay of Plenty's volcanic coastline is perfectly suited to it. No climbing or swimming expertise is needed, just a willingness to get wet and push your comfort zone.

reviews.summary: The Ohope Beach Coasteering adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Scramble, swim, and leap off cliffs along the volcanic coastline at Ohope. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Mount Brewing Co
pois.short: Mount Maunganui craft brewery with a relaxed taproom — hop-forward ales and beach-town vibes.
pois.commentary: Mount Brewing Co captures the laid-back Mount Maunganui lifestyle in liquid form. The taproom pours a rotating selection of craft beers — IPAs, pale ales and sessionable lagers — alongside local guest taps. The outdoor seating area is perfect for a post-beach beer, and the brewery is a short walk from the Main Beach. Tasting paddles are good value for sampling the range.

reviews.summary: Good beer in a great location. The relaxed vibe matches the Mount lifestyle. Some find the range limited compared to bigger breweries.

pois.name: Alpino Restaurant & Bar
pois.short: Mount Maunganui's award-winning Italian — premium ingredients, ocean views and outstanding hospitality.
pois.commentary: Alpino has won multiple hospitality awards for its approach to Italian-inspired cooking using premium local ingredients. The Pacific Avenue location captures sea views, and the menu ranges from exceptional pasta to seasonal seafood. The bar offers craft cocktails and a curated wine list. Both the café (daytime) and restaurant (evening) experiences are excellent. A cut above the typical beach-town restaurant.

reviews.summary: Consistently rated Mount Maunganui's best restaurant. The Italian-NZ fusion works beautifully. Book ahead for dinner — it fills fast, especially in summer.

pois.name: Tauranga Farmers' Market
pois.short: Saturday morning market at Tauranga Primary School — 60+ stalls of BOP produce, baking and street food.
pois.commentary: Every Saturday morning the Tauranga Primary School grounds host one of the Bay of Plenty's best farmers' markets. Over 60 stalls sell seasonal produce — avocados, kiwifruit, berries, citrus — alongside artisan breads, cheeses, smoked meats and street food. The BOP's mild climate means excellent produce year-round. The coffee is good and the atmosphere is relaxed and family-friendly.

reviews.summary: Great variety of local produce, especially the subtropical fruits. The street food stalls are popular. Parking near the school can be tricky — arrive early.

pois.name: Fife Lane Kitchen & Bar
pois.short: Mount Maunganui izakaya blending Māori and Japanese culinary traditions — creative small plates and sake.
pois.commentary: Fife Lane is one of the most original restaurants in the Bay of Plenty, blending Māori and Japanese food cultures in an izakaya ('stay and drink') format. Expect small plates that riff on both traditions — think hangi-smoked meats with Japanese seasonings, local seafood with wasabi, and creative cocktails. The atmosphere is convivial and the concept is genuinely unique in New Zealand.

reviews.summary: The Māori-Japanese concept is praised as genuinely creative. The small plates are perfect for sharing. Some find the portions small for the price.

pois.name: Ōhope Beach Seafood
pois.short: Ōhope's best fish and chips — fresh local catch on one of New Zealand's most beautiful beaches.
pois.commentary: Ōhope Beach is regularly voted one of New Zealand's best beaches, and the fish and chip shops here use the freshest local catch — snapper, blue cod, tarakihi. Grab a portion, find a spot on the sand, and eat while watching the sunset over White Island. It's the quintessential New Zealand beach experience. Simple, unpretentious, and better than it has any right to be.

reviews.summary: Fresh fish and a beautiful beach — hard to go wrong. Nothing fancy, just excellent fish and chips. Best visited on a fine evening for the full experience.

pois.name: Mills Reef Winery & Restaurant
pois.short: BOP's premier winery — cellar door tastings, vineyard restaurant and award-winning Elspeth wines.
pois.commentary: Mills Reef is the Bay of Plenty's standout winery, producing wines from both local and Hawke's Bay grapes. The Elspeth range — particularly the Syrah and Chardonnay — consistently wins gold medals. The vineyard restaurant serves seasonal local produce in an elegant setting, and the cellar door offers comprehensive tastings. It's a short drive from Tauranga and makes a great lunch destination.

reviews.summary: The restaurant and cellar door combination is well-executed. The Elspeth wines impress. Some note it's not technically a vineyard at the site, as grapes come from Hawke's Bay.

pois.name: Astrolabe Brew Bar
pois.short: Mount Maunganui gastropub — craft beers, wood-fired pizzas, burgers and a buzzy atmosphere.
pois.commentary: Astrolabe is the Mount's go-to for craft beer and pub food. The tap selection covers local and NZ craft breweries, and the wood-fired pizzas, burgers and seafood are consistently good. The outdoor area catches the afternoon sun, making it popular for post-beach gatherings. Regular live music and events add to the atmosphere. Family-friendly during the day, livelier at night.

reviews.summary: Solid beer selection and good pub food. The atmosphere is fun and social. Can get noisy and crowded on weekend evenings.

pois.name: Comvita Visitor Centre
pois.short: World-famous Mānuka honey at the source — tastings, education and the Comvita story in Paengaroa.
pois.commentary: Comvita is one of the world's most recognised Mānuka honey brands, and their visitor centre in Paengaroa (near Te Puke) tells the story of this remarkable NZ product. Interactive displays explain the science behind UMF ratings, and you can taste different grades of Mānuka honey. The gift shop sells products at better prices than airports. A unique food experience that connects you to one of NZ's most valuable exports.

reviews.summary: Educational and interesting for honey enthusiasts. The tastings help understand the UMF system. Some find it commercial, but the product quality is undeniable.

pois.name: Picnicka
pois.short: Tauranga CBD sharing-plates restaurant — seasonal BOP produce, house-made everything, and a buzzing atmosphere.
pois.commentary: Picnicka sits in Tauranga's revitalised CBD and has quickly become the city's most exciting restaurant. The sharing-plates format showcases Bay of Plenty produce — expect local seafood, seasonal vegetables and house-made everything from bread to ice cream. The natural wine list and cocktail programme are equally creative. A bright spot in a city whose dining scene is rapidly improving.

reviews.summary: The most exciting restaurant in Tauranga's CBD. The sharing format works well. Some find the small-plates pricing adds up quickly.

pois.name: The Barrel Room (Tauranga)
pois.short: Tauranga's popular wine and beer bar — NZ wines by the glass, craft taps and sharing platters.
pois.commentary: The Barrel Room fills a gap in Tauranga's social scene with a curated selection of NZ wines by the glass, rotating craft beer taps and sharing platters of local produce. The atmosphere is relaxed and social — part wine bar, part neighbourhood hangout. The staff are knowledgeable about wine and happy to guide you through the selection. A good evening option after a day at the beach.

reviews.summary: A welcome addition to Tauranga's evening scene. The wine-by-the-glass selection is well-curated. Some find the food secondary to the drinks.

pois.name: Bobby's Fresh Fish Market
pois.short: Tauranga wharf-side fish market — the freshest catch straight off the boats, plus fish 'n' chips.
pois.commentary: Bobby's sits on the Tauranga wharf where fishing boats unload their catch. The fish display showcases whatever's come in that day — snapper, tarakihi, hapuku, John Dory — and you can buy it fresh to cook or have it battered and fried on the spot. The fish and chips, made with hours-fresh fish, are outstanding. Seating is basic (wharf benches), but the freshness makes up for any lack of ambiance.

reviews.summary: Fish doesn't get fresher than this. The fish and chips are excellent. Basic setup — it's about the seafood, not the décor.

pois.name: Ōhiwa Harbour Wildlife Refuge
pois.short: One of New Zealand's most important estuaries for wading birds — dotterel, oystercatcher, and godwit.
pois.commentary: Ōhiwa Harbour is a shallow tidal estuary between Whakatāne and Ōpōtiki, rated as one of NZ's top five estuaries for shorebird populations. NZ dotterel, variable oystercatcher, and bar-tailed godwit are resident, with Arctic migrants arriving each summer.

reviews.summary: Outstanding birding. Best at low tide. The harbour itself is beautiful and uncrowded.

pois.name: Whale Island / Moutohorā
pois.short: Predator-free island nature reserve — tuatara, saddleback, and red-crowned kākāriki thrive here.
pois.commentary: Moutohorā is a 143-hectare island off Whakatāne, cleared of pests and now home to translocated tuatara, North Island saddleback, red-crowned kākāriki, and little spotted kiwi. DOC runs limited guided visits during summer.

reviews.summary: Incredible wildlife experience. Very limited access (ballot system). The tuatara viewing alone is worth the trip.

pois.name: Maketū Estuary — Kaituna Wetland
pois.short: Restored wetland where the Kaituna River meets the sea — bittern, fernbird, and royal spoonbill.
pois.commentary: The Kaituna River re-diversion in 2018 reconnected the Maketū Estuary to its natural water supply, restoring critical habitat. The wetlands now support Australasian bittern, fernbird, spotless crake, and growing numbers of royal spoonbill.

reviews.summary: Great restoration success story. Boardwalk makes access easy. Best for birding at dawn.

pois.name: Kiwi360 — Native Bird Walk
pois.short: Beyond the kiwifruit, a native bird trail through regenerating bush with kererū, tūī, and bellbird.
pois.commentary: While primarily a kiwifruit attraction, Kiwi360 in Te Puke has developed a native bird trail through its regenerating bush block. The pest-control programme has brought back good numbers of kererū, tūī, and bellbird. A family-friendly introduction to NZ bush birds.

reviews.summary: Fun for families. Combines the kiwifruit experience with some nice bush birding.

pois.name: Pāpāmoa Beach Dotterel Nesting
pois.short: One of the most important NZ dotterel nesting beaches — volunteer wardens protect nests each spring.
pois.commentary: Pāpāmoa Beach hosts one of the Bay of Plenty's largest NZ dotterel breeding populations. During spring and summer, volunteer wardens monitor and protect nests from predators and disturbance. The rare shore plover has also been sighted here.

reviews.summary: Great conservation effort. Keep dogs on leads during nesting season. Beautiful beach regardless.

pois.name: Whakaari Seabird Coast
pois.short: Boat trips past seabird colonies — grey-faced petrel, Australasian gannet, and shearwater breeding grounds.
pois.commentary: The coastal waters between Whakatāne and White Island host significant seabird colonies. Boat tours pass grey-faced petrel nesting cliffs, Australasian gannet colonies, and in the right season, hundreds of thousands of sooty shearwaters heading to their breeding islands.

reviews.summary: Spectacular seabird viewing. Best combined with a White Island scenic cruise. Bring binoculars.

pois.name: McLaren Falls Glowworm Walk
pois.short: Free glowworm walk through native bush — thousands of tiny lights along the stream bank after dark.
pois.commentary: McLaren Falls Park near Tauranga hosts a free glowworm walk along a bush-lined stream. After dark, the banks light up with thousands of Arachnocampa luminosa. No booking needed — just bring a torch for the walk in, then turn it off at the viewing area.

reviews.summary: Amazing free experience. Best on dark, humid nights. Gets busy in summer so arrive early.

pois.name: Marine Education Tauranga — Reef Check
pois.short: Citizen science reef monitoring programme — join a guided snorkel survey of Tauranga's reef systems.
pois.commentary: Reef Check NZ runs guided snorkel surveys along the Tauranga coast where volunteers help monitor reef health. Learn to identify NZ marine species and contribute to real conservation data. Sessions run from November to March.

reviews.summary: Excellent marine education experience. Great for older kids and adults. Book ahead in summer.

pois.name: Aongatete Forest — Predator Control Zone
pois.short: Community-maintained pest control in Kaimai bush — excellent for kererū, kākā, and North Island robin.
pois.commentary: The Aongatete Forest Restoration Trust has maintained a trapping network since 2004, dramatically reducing predator numbers. The result: kererū are everywhere, kākā have returned, and North Island robin can be seen along the walking tracks. The loop track is moderate and well-maintained.

reviews.summary: Superb bush birding. The trapping programme has made a visible difference. Robins are very tame and curious.

pois.name: Little Waihi Estuary — Wetland Walk
pois.short: Quiet estuary boardwalk with royal spoonbill, white-faced heron, and seasonal wading birds.
pois.commentary: The Little Waihi Estuary boardwalk provides easy viewing of a productive tidal wetland. Royal spoonbill, white-faced heron, pied stilt, and kingfisher are year-round residents. In summer, godwits and knots arrive from the Arctic.

reviews.summary: Peaceful walk with good bird variety. Boardwalk is pushchair-friendly. Best at low tide.

pois.name: Welcome Bay Bush — Kiwi Listening
pois.short: Community pest-control success — North Island brown kiwi can now be heard calling in the bush above Welcome Bay.
pois.commentary: Intensive community trapping in the hills above Welcome Bay has allowed kiwi to return to areas where they hadn't been heard for decades. Evening listening walks from the bush edge can hear kiwi calling, especially during winter and spring.

reviews.summary: Exciting to hear kiwi so close to the city. Best in winter evenings. Join a guided listening event.

pois.name: Mount Maunganui — Little Blue Penguin Colony
pois.short: Little blue penguins nest in the rocks around the base of Mauao — watch them come ashore at dusk.
pois.commentary: A small colony of kororā (little blue penguins) nests in rock crevices around the base of Mauao / Mount Maunganui. At dusk, especially between September and February, you can watch them waddle ashore. Keep your distance and no torches.

reviews.summary: Delightful surprise in an urban setting. Very dependent on timing and luck. Best at dusk during breeding season.

pois.name: Ōkere Falls — Giant Longfin Eel Viewing
pois.short: Watch giant longfin eels — some over 100 years old — in the crystal-clear pools below Ōkere Falls.
pois.commentary: The pools at the base of Ōkere Falls on the Kaituna River are home to giant longfin eels (tuna), some estimated to be over a century old. These ancient creatures are endemic to NZ and increasingly rare. A short walk from the car park leads to viewing spots.

reviews.summary: Fascinating to see these ancient creatures up close. Short, easy walk. Combine with the Kaituna rafting.

pois.name: Tauranga Art Gallery Toi Tauranga
pois.short: Dynamic contemporary art gallery in central Tauranga showcasing national and international exhibitions.
pois.commentary: Tauranga Art Gallery Toi Tauranga is the Bay of Plenty's premier public art gallery, located in the heart of Tauranga's CBD. The gallery presents a rotating programme of contemporary art exhibitions featuring both established and emerging New Zealand artists alongside international shows. The building itself is a converted heritage structure modernised with striking gallery spaces. Exhibitions change regularly, ensuring there is always something new to see. The gallery runs an active education and events programme including artist talks, workshops, and holiday programmes for children. Admission is free, making it an easy addition to any Tauranga visit. The central location means it is surrounded by good cafés and within walking distance of the waterfront. The gallery shop stocks quality art books, prints, and locally made jewellery and ceramics.

reviews.summary: A surprisingly good gallery for a regional centre. Exhibitions are well curated and always changing. Free entry makes it a great rainy-day option in Tauranga.

pois.name: Baycourt Community & Arts Centre
pois.short: Tauranga's main performing arts venue hosting theatre, music, comedy, and community events year-round.
pois.commentary: Baycourt Community & Arts Centre is Tauranga's primary performing arts venue, situated in the central city. The complex includes the Addison Theatre (a 498-seat auditorium), the more intimate X Space for experimental works, and community meeting rooms. Baycourt hosts a diverse programme of touring shows, local theatre productions, live music, comedy acts, dance performances, and community events throughout the year. The venue plays a vital role in the Bay of Plenty's cultural life, with regular seasons from local companies and visiting national acts. The Addison Theatre was refurbished in recent years with improved acoustics and comfort. Check the online calendar for current shows and look for discounted tickets to preview nights. The central location means excellent dining options are just steps away on Devonport Road and the waterfront.

reviews.summary: A solid regional theatre with a good mix of touring and local shows. The refurbished auditorium is comfortable with decent acoustics. Great to have quality performing arts in Tauranga.

pois.name: Paeroa L&P Bottle
pois.short: Iconic giant L&P bottle — a classic New Zealand roadside photo stop celebrating the 'World Famous in NZ' drink.
pois.commentary: The giant Lemon & Paeroa bottle is one of New Zealand's most iconic roadside attractions, standing in the centre of Paeroa township in the Hauraki Plains. L&P, marketed as 'World Famous in New Zealand,' was originally made from natural mineral water from Paeroa's springs combined with lemon juice, first commercially produced in 1907. The oversized bottle sculpture has been a compulsory photo stop for Kiwi road-trippers for decades and represents New Zealand's fondness for quirky 'Big Things.' While the drink is now manufactured elsewhere, Paeroa proudly maintains its claim as the home of L&P. The town itself is worth a brief stop for its antique shops along the main street. Paeroa sits on State Highway 2 between Auckland and Tauranga, making it a natural rest stop. Pull over, take the obligatory photo, and grab a cold L&P from the nearby dairy.

reviews.summary: It's a classic Kiwi road trip stop — you have to get the photo! Not much to do beyond the quick snap, but the town has some decent antique shops. Grab an L&P for the road.

pois.name: Mount Maunganui Main Beach
pois.short: New Zealand's premier ocean beach with consistent surf, golden sand, and iconic Mauao volcano backdrop.
pois.commentary: Mount Maunganui Main Beach is consistently rated one of New Zealand's best beaches, a sweeping arc of golden sand stretching from the base of Mauao (the Mount) along the ocean side of the tombolo. The beach offers excellent surf conditions with reliable swells, making it hugely popular with surfers of all levels. The northern end near the Mount has the best breaks, while the gentler southern stretch suits swimmers and families. The beach is patrolled by surf lifeguards during summer. Behind the beach, the Mount's vibrant strip of cafés, restaurants, and surf shops creates a classic beach-town atmosphere. The beach can be combined with the 45-minute walk around the base of Mauao or the steeper summit track for panoramic views. Hot salt-water pools at the base of the Mount offer a perfect post-surf soak. Summer weekends are busy — arrive early for parking.

reviews.summary: Simply the best beach in New Zealand. Gorgeous golden sand, great surf, and Mauao towering above. The whole Mount area has such a fantastic beach-town vibe with awesome cafés and the hot pools.

pois.name: Kohi Point Walkway / Whakatāne Heads
pois.short: Spectacular coastal walkway with pōhutukawa-lined cliffs, views to Whakaari, and NZ fur seal colonies.
pois.commentary: The Kohi Point Walkway is a stunning coastal track that traverses the dramatic headland at Whakatāne Heads, on the eastern Bay of Plenty coast. The walk passes through groves of ancient pōhutukawa trees clinging to steep cliffs above the sea, with expansive views across the Bay of Plenty to Whakaari (White Island) and Moutohora (Whale Island). New Zealand fur seals frequently haul out on the rocks below — bring binoculars. The headland is of great significance to local Māori, being the traditional landing place of the Mataatua waka. The walk takes about 1.5 to 2 hours return and can be accessed from the western end near the Whakatāne Heads lookout or from Ōhope Beach on the eastern side. The track is well formed but includes some steps and moderate climbs. Best on a clear day for volcanic island views.

reviews.summary: One of the best short coastal walks in the Bay of Plenty. The pōhutukawa trees and seal colonies are highlights. Views to White Island on a clear day are incredible.

pois.name: Waihi Beach
pois.short: Nine kilometres of golden sand beach, perfect for surfing, swimming, and relaxed family beach holidays.
pois.commentary: Waihi Beach is a magnificent nine-kilometre stretch of golden sand on the western Bay of Plenty coast, regarded as one of New Zealand's finest surf beaches. The northern end near the main village is patrolled during summer and offers good swimming, while surfers head further south toward Bowentown for more consistent breaks. The beach is backed by a small, laid-back holiday village with cafés, surf shops, and a general store. At the southern end, the Orokawa Bay Track provides a rewarding bush walk to a secluded beach. The Bowentown headland offers spectacular views and historic pā sites. Waihi Beach has a strong community of surfers and retirees, giving it a relaxed, unhurried atmosphere quite different from the busier Mount Maunganui. The campground right behind the beach fills quickly over Christmas and January — book well ahead.

reviews.summary: A beautiful, slightly quieter alternative to the Mount with great surf and a gorgeous long stretch of sand. The walk to Orokawa Bay is a hidden gem. Lovely relaxed holiday atmosphere.

pois.name: Comvita Visitor Centre
pois.short: World-leading mānuka honey experience with tastings, bee education, and premium honey products.
pois.commentary: The Comvita Visitor Centre in Paengaroa offers an immersive experience into the world of New Zealand mānuka honey, one of the country's most prized natural products. Comvita is a global leader in mānuka honey and bee products, founded in the Bay of Plenty in 1974. The visitor centre features interactive displays explaining the unique properties of mānuka honey, the science behind its antibacterial UMF rating, and the lifecycle of honeybees. Guided tastings allow visitors to compare different grades of mānuka honey and sample honey-infused products. The on-site shop offers the full Comvita range, often at better prices than retail, including premium UMF-rated honey, propolis products, and skincare. The centre is located on State Highway 33 between Tauranga and Rotorua, making it a natural stop between the two destinations. Allow about an hour for the full experience.

reviews.summary: Fascinating insight into mānuka honey production with generous tastings. Great place to buy premium honey at competitive prices. A worthwhile stop on the Tauranga to Rotorua drive.

pois.name: Mount Brewing Co
pois.short: Craft brewery taproom at the foot of Mauao in Mount Maunganui with locally inspired beers.
pois.commentary: Mount Brewing Co operates from a characterful taproom at the base of Mauao (Mount Maunganui), producing small-batch beers inspired by the Bay of Plenty lifestyle. Their core range includes a refreshing Pacific Pale Ale and a smooth Pilot Bay Pilsner, with rotating seasonal and experimental brews on tap. The indoor-outdoor space has a relaxed surf-town feel with communal tables, perfect for post-beach refreshment. Food trucks frequently park outside, and the brewery is a short walk from the main beach and the base track up Mauao. The brewing team draws on both traditional and modern techniques, and the taproom offers tastings and takeaway fills. Check their social media for live music nights and special release events.

reviews.summary: A great spot for craft beer after a day at the beach or a climb up the Mount. The rotating tap list keeps things interesting. The laid-back atmosphere matches the Mount Maunganui vibe perfectly.

pois.name: Waimarino Adventure Park
pois.short: Waterfront adventure park on Waimarino Stream with kayaking, paddleboarding, and the Blob water launcher.
pois.commentary: Waimarino Adventure Park is a summer favourite on the banks of Waimarino Stream near Bethlehem, Tauranga. The park offers a wide range of water-based activities including kayaking, stand-up paddleboarding, and the famous Blob, an inflatable launcher that catapults riders into the water. A network of floating platforms, water trampolines, and slides creates an aquatic playground suitable for families and groups. On-land activities include mini golf and a climbing wall. The sheltered waterway provides calm conditions ideal for beginners, and life jackets are provided. Picnic areas and a cafe make it easy to spend a full day. The park operates seasonally from October to April, and booking online is recommended during school holidays when it gets very busy.

reviews.summary: Families and groups love the variety of water activities and the iconic Blob experience. Great value for a full day of fun. Can get crowded during peak summer weekends.

pois.name: Katikati Mural Town
pois.short: New Zealand's mural town with over 40 outdoor artworks depicting the community's history and culture.
pois.commentary: Katikati bills itself as the Mural Town of New Zealand, with more than 40 large-scale outdoor murals painted on buildings throughout the main street and surrounding area. The mural project began in 1991 and each artwork tells a story of the town's history, from early Maori settlement to European immigration and the local fruit-growing industry. A self-guided walking trail map is available from the information centre, allowing visitors to discover the murals at their own pace. Notable works include scenes of the town's Ulster settler origins and the evolution of the kiwifruit industry. The nearby Katikati Bird Gardens and Haiku Pathway along the Uretara Stream add to the town's creative character. Located on SH2 between Tauranga and Waihi, it makes an interesting stop on a Bay of Plenty road trip.

reviews.summary: A charming small-town stop with impressive mural art. The self-guided walk takes about an hour. A pleasant surprise for those passing through on the highway.

pois.name: Maketu Pies
pois.short: Legendary New Zealand pie shop in Maketu, famous for its award-winning mince and cheese and seafood pies.
pois.commentary: Maketu Pies is a New Zealand food institution, a roadside pie shop that has been producing award-winning pies in the small Bay of Plenty settlement of Maketu since 1985. The shop is famous for its classic mince and cheese pie, voted among New Zealand's best at multiple pie awards, and its specialty seafood pies featuring locally caught fish and shellfish. The pies are made fresh daily using traditional recipes and quality ingredients. Locals and travellers queue regularly, especially during holiday weekends, and the pies have become a must-stop for anyone driving the Bay of Plenty coast. The shop also sells sausage rolls, filled rolls, and other bakery items. Maketu itself is a historic settlement at the mouth of the Kaituna River, significant as the landing place of the Te Arawa waka. Eating a Maketu pie on the beach watching the river meet the sea is a quintessentially Kiwi experience.

reviews.summary: The pies truly live up to their legendary reputation. The mince and cheese is perfection and the seafood options are a treat. Worth the detour off the main highway.

pois.name: Whakaari White Island Scenic Flights
pois.short: Scenic helicopter or fixed-wing flights over Whakaari/White Island, New Zealand's most active marine volcano.
pois.commentary: Whakaari/White Island is New Zealand's most active marine volcano, rising dramatically from the sea 48 kilometres off the Bay of Plenty coast. Following the tragic 2019 eruption, landing tours have ceased, but scenic flights by helicopter and fixed-wing aircraft offer spectacular aerial views of the steaming volcanic crater, bright yellow sulphur deposits, and the dramatic coastline. Flights depart from Whakatane and Tauranga airports, circling the island to provide views from multiple angles. The volcanic activity is clearly visible from the air, with steam and gas plumes rising from multiple vents. The flights also offer views of the Bay of Plenty coastline, Moutohora Island, and the wider volcanic landscape. Flight duration varies from 30 minutes to over an hour depending on the operator and route. Conditions can be changeable and flights are weather dependent. An awe-inspiring perspective on New Zealand's volcanic heritage.

reviews.summary: A dramatic aerial experience offering views of an active volcano that cannot be accessed any other way. The volcanic activity is mesmerising from above. Weather dependent, so flexibility helps.

pois.name: Kaituna River White Water Rafting
pois.short: White water rafting on the Kaituna River including a 7-metre drop over Tutea Falls, the highest rafted waterfall.
pois.commentary: The Kaituna River near Rotorua and the Bay of Plenty offers one of the world's most exhilarating white water rafting experiences, featuring Tutea Falls, a 7-metre vertical waterfall that is the highest commercially rafted waterfall in the world. The Grade 5 river provides intense rapids through a bush-lined gorge, with the Tutea Falls drop the undeniable highlight. Experienced guides navigate the inflatable raft through a series of rapids before the dramatic plunge over the falls. No prior rafting experience is required, but participants must be comfortable in water and reasonably fit. Full wetsuits, helmets, and life jackets are provided. The total trip lasts around 2.5 hours with about 50 minutes on the water. Multiple operators run trips daily, departing from Rotorua or the Okere Falls area. Minimum age is typically 13 years. The adrenaline rush of going over Tutea Falls is unforgettable.

reviews.summary: An absolutely thrilling rafting experience with the world's highest commercially rafted waterfall. The Tutea Falls drop is terrifying and exhilarating. Guides are skilled and keep everyone safe while maximising the fun.

pois.name: McLaren Falls Park
pois.short: Scenic park near Tauranga with waterfalls, a lake, and free glowworm viewing along a bush-lined stream.
pois.commentary: McLaren Falls Park is a 190-hectare regional park situated between Tauranga and Taupo, offering a combination of scenic gardens, native bush, waterfalls, and one of the Bay of Plenty's best-kept secrets: free glowworm viewing. After dark, a short walk along the stream near the park entrance reveals thousands of native glowworms (titiwai) lining the banks, creating a free natural light show rivalling commercial glowworm attractions. During the day, the park offers swimming in the lake, walking tracks through native bush and exotic gardens, picnic areas, and the photogenic McLaren Falls. The park is popular for events including outdoor concerts and festivals. Camping is available at a designated campground. Located on SH29 about 20 minutes from Tauranga, the park is easily accessible for evening glowworm visits. Bring a torch for the walk in but switch it off to see the glowworms. Best viewed on dark, still nights away from full moon.

reviews.summary: A wonderful free attraction with impressive glowworm displays after dark. The daytime park is also beautiful with good walking tracks. A hidden gem that many visitors miss.

pois.name: White Island Tours / Whakaari Boat Trip
pois.short: Scenic boat trip to view Whakaari / White Island active volcano.
pois.commentary: Whakaari / White Island is New Zealand's most active cone volcano, sitting forty-nine kilometres offshore in the Bay of Plenty. While island landings have been suspended since the 2019 eruption, boat tours from Whakatane offer close-up views of the steaming volcanic island from the water. The journey crosses open sea and regularly encounters dolphins, seabirds, and occasionally whales. The island's scarred landscape, with steam and gas venting from multiple points, is a vivid reminder of the Earth's volcanic power. Tours typically last four to five hours and include commentary on the island's geological and human history. Sea conditions can cause cancellations, so flexible scheduling is advisable. The experience is both thrilling and sobering.

reviews.summary: A powerful experience viewing an active volcano from the water. The dolphin encounters are a bonus. Be prepared for sea conditions.

pois.name: Mauao / Mount Maunganui Summit
pois.short: The iconic summit walk — spectacular 360° views over harbour, coast, and ocean from a sacred volcanic peak.
pois.commentary: Mauao's summit walk is the quintessential Bay of Plenty experience. The one-hour loop climbs through native bush to 232 metres, rewarding you with sweeping views over Tauranga Harbour, the Pacific coastline, and on clear days, all the way to the Coromandel Peninsula and White Island. The mountain is sacred to local iwi Ngāi Te Rangi — its name means 'caught by the dawn.' Early morning walks beat the crowds and catch the sunrise. The base track offers a gentler alternative with coastal views throughout.

reviews.summary: Universally praised as the must-do Bay of Plenty walk. The sunrise summit experience is rated exceptional. Well-maintained tracks suit all fitness levels.

pois.name: Karangahake Gorge Historic Walkway
pois.short: Walk through mining tunnels and across bridges in a dramatic river gorge — NZ's finest goldfield heritage walk.
pois.commentary: Karangahake Gorge cuts through the Kaimai Range where the Ōhinemuri River has carved a narrow passage through solid rock. Gold was discovered here in 1875, and the gorge became one of New Zealand's richest goldfields. The Windows Walk is the highlight — a tunnel carved through the cliff face with openings (windows) overlooking the gorge below. Swing bridges, old battery ruins, and railway tunnels complete the picture. The full circuit takes about three hours, but the Windows Walk alone is an unforgettable one-hour loop.

reviews.summary: Walkers rate the Windows Walk as one of the best short walks in the North Island. The combination of history, geology, and river scenery is praised. Well-signposted and accessible year-round.

pois.name: Te Puke Kiwifruit Country
pois.short: The kiwifruit capital of the world — orchard tours and tastings in the heart of NZ's biggest export crop.
pois.commentary: Te Puke produces more kiwifruit than anywhere else on the planet, and the industry is worth over $3 billion annually to New Zealand. Orchard tours let you walk among the vines, taste gold and green varieties fresh from the plant, and learn how a Chinese gooseberry became New Zealand's signature fruit. The giant kiwifruit slice on the main road is one of New Zealand's great roadside icons. Visit between March and June for harvest season when the orchards are at their busiest and most photogenic.

reviews.summary: Visitors enjoy learning the kiwifruit story and tasting varieties unavailable overseas. The giant kiwifruit photo stop is a crowd favourite. Some find the tours brief for the price.

pois.name: McLaren Falls Park
pois.short: Beautiful park with lake, waterfall, and an incredible glow-worm display on the night walk.
pois.commentary: McLaren Falls Park is a 190-hectare reserve straddling the Omanawa River between Tauranga and the Kaimai Range. By day, the lake, waterfall, and bush walks are the draw — native birds are abundant and the gardens are beautifully maintained. But the real magic happens after dark: the glow-worm walk along the stream banks reveals one of the most concentrated natural light shows in the North Island. Bring a torch with a red filter, and be prepared for genuine wonder. The park is also popular for kayaking and picnics.

reviews.summary: The glow-worm night walk is rated as a must-do Bay of Plenty experience. Daytime visitors enjoy the peaceful lake and bush setting. Free entry makes it exceptional value.

pois.name: Whakaari / White Island
pois.short: NZ's most active marine volcano — accessible by scenic flight for dramatic views of the crater and gas plumes.
pois.commentary: Whakaari / White Island sits 48km off the Bay of Plenty coast — New Zealand's most active cone volcano, continuously venting steam and gas. Following the tragic 2019 eruption, island landing tours are suspended, but scenic flights and boat tours offer extraordinary views of the steaming crater, acid lake, and sulphur-stained cliffs from a safe distance. The volcano's raw power is humbling. Whakaari is a private island owned by the Buttle family since 1936, with a complex history of sulphur mining and volcanic monitoring.

reviews.summary: Scenic flights over the volcano are rated as awe-inspiring. The 2019 eruption context adds solemnity to the experience. Weather can cancel flights — book flexible dates.

pois.name: Maketu Estuary & Historic Village
pois.short: Where the Te Arawa waka landed around 1350 — rich Māori history and excellent birdlife at the estuary.
pois.commentary: Maketu holds a special place in New Zealand history as the landing site of the Te Arawa waka, one of the great voyaging canoes that brought Polynesian settlers to Aotearoa around 1350. The village remains an important Te Arawa community today. The estuary is a significant wetland for wading birds, with boardwalks offering excellent birdwatching. Historic plaques and monuments tell the story of arrival and settlement. While you're here, try the famous Maketu pies — a local institution since 1949.

reviews.summary: Visitors value the deep Māori history and the peaceful estuary setting. Maketu pies are mentioned in almost every review. A quieter, more reflective alternative to the busy Mount.

pois.name: Papamoa Hills Regional Park
pois.short: Walk through ancient Māori pā terraces with sweeping coastal views from the summit.
pois.commentary: Papamoa Hills Regional Park offers a superb hill walk through ancient pā site terraces with panoramic views of the Bay of Plenty coastline. The terraced earthworks of the former Māori fortifications are clearly visible on the hillside — a reminder that this was a strategically important site for centuries before European arrival. The summit loop takes about two hours and crosses farmland and regenerating bush, with views stretching from Mount Maunganui to Whakatāne. It's a moderately steep climb, but the 360° views from the top reward every step.

reviews.summary: Walkers praise the combination of Māori heritage and coastal views. The pā terraces are clearly visible and well-interpreted. A rewarding walk that most tourists overlook.

pois.name: Wairere Falls
pois.short: North Island's highest waterfall at 153m — a rewarding bush walk through the Kaimai Range.
pois.commentary: Wairere Falls is the North Island's tallest waterfall, plunging 153 metres down a sheer rock face in the Kaimai Range. The track to the base lookout takes about 45 minutes through beautiful native bush. For the adventurous, a steeper track continues to the top of the falls for a vertigo-inducing view straight down the drop. The full return walk takes three to four hours. The bush is home to kererū, tūī, and fantails, and the forest canopy is dense enough to keep the track shaded on hot days. Best after rain when the falls are at full volume.

reviews.summary: Trampers rate the top-of-falls viewpoint as genuinely thrilling. The bush walk is beautiful in its own right. The track can be muddy and steep — proper footwear essential.

pois.name: Mount Brewing Co & BOP Craft Beer
pois.short: Craft beer scene centred on Mount Maunganui — brewing excellence with ocean views.
pois.commentary: The Bay of Plenty's craft beer scene has exploded in recent years, with Mount Brewing Co leading the charge from its taproom near the Mount main beach. Their hoppy pale ales and session IPAs are brewed to match the beach lifestyle — crisp, refreshing, and designed for long summer afternoons. The wider BOP scene includes Brewbus tours connecting multiple breweries across Tauranga and the Mount. The combination of fresh beer and ocean proximity is hard to beat anywhere in New Zealand.

reviews.summary: Beer lovers praise the quality and variety of the local craft scene. The beach-brewery combination is rated unique in NZ. Taproom hours can be limited — check before visiting.

pois.name: McLaren Falls Park
pois.short: A waterfall park by day, a glow-worm wonderland by night.
pois.commentary: The banks of the stream light up with thousands of glow-worms after dark. A free bioluminescence display rivalling Waitomo caves.

reviews.summary: The free glow-worm display is genuinely spectacular.

pois.name: Whakaari / White Island
pois.short: NZ’s most active volcano — a dramatic cone rising from the Bay of Plenty.
pois.commentary: Following the 2019 eruption that killed 22 people, landing tours have been suspended. Boat and aerial viewing tours continue.

reviews.summary: The volcanic scenery is dramatic even from boat distance.

pois.name: The Elms / Te Papa Mission Station
pois.short: The oldest mission station in the Bay of Plenty — an 1835 complex.
pois.commentary: Established in 1835, includes what is believed to be NZ’s oldest surviving wooden library (1839). Heritage NZ Category 1.

reviews.summary: A peaceful heritage oasis in the middle of modern Tauranga.

pois.name: Mount Maunganui / Mauao
pois.short: A volcanic cone, a world-class surf beach and hot salt-water pools.
pois.commentary: Mauao is a 232-metre extinct volcanic cone. The summit walk takes 45 minutes. The main beach is one of NZ’s best surf breaks. Hot salt-water pools at the base.

reviews.summary: The complete beach experience — surf, swim, walk the mount, soak in the hot pools.

pois.name: Papamoa Hills Cultural Heritage Regional Park
pois.short: A 135-hectare regional park above the Bay of Plenty coast preserving ancient Maori pa sites with sweeping views from Mauao to Whakatane.
pois.commentary: Te Rae o Papamoa sits above the Papamoa coastal strip and reveals one of the most significant concentrations of Maori pa fortifications in the Bay of Plenty, carved into the hill terraces by tangata whenua over many centuries. The park's elevated ridgeline provides extraordinary views south to Mauao (Mount Maunganui), east along the coast toward Whakatane, and inland to the Kaimai Ranges. Several loop tracks of varying length traverse pine forest and open farmland, passing interpretation panels that explain the historic significance of the earthworks still clearly visible underfoot. Sunrise visits are particularly rewarding, when coastal mist fills the valleys below and the volcanic coast is lit in golden light.

reviews.summary: Visitors appreciate the combination of accessible hillside walking and genuine Maori heritage, with the panoramic coastal views consistently described as among the best in the Bay of Plenty. Less crowded than nearby Mauao.

pois.name: Mataatua — The House That Came Home
pois.short: An 1875 meeting house that travelled to museums in Sydney, Melbourne, and London before being repatriated to its Whakatane birthplace in 2011.
pois.commentary: Mataatua Wharenui was built in 1875 for the Ngati Awa people and was one of the finest examples of traditional Maori carving and architecture of its era. Within a year of completion it was shipped to the Sydney International Exhibition of 1879 and spent more than a century in various overseas and New Zealand museums, becoming a powerful symbol of the colonial removal of Maori taonga. Its return to Whakatane in 2011 and subsequent reconstruction on the Wairaka marae represents one of New Zealand's most significant cultural repatriations. Guided tours delivered by Ngati Awa people themselves tell the full story of the house's extraordinary journey and the living culture it embodies.

reviews.summary: Reviewers describe the Mataatua experience as deeply moving and unlike any other cultural attraction in New Zealand, with the story of repatriation giving the carvings and tukutuku panels a weight and resonance that profoundly affects visitors.

pois.name: The Elms Mission House and Garden
pois.short: One of New Zealand's oldest surviving European buildings, a gracious mid-1800s mission house set in a heritage garden in central Tauranga.
pois.commentary: The Elms, built in 1847 for the Reverend Alfred Brown, is one of the few remaining pre-Land Wars European buildings in the Bay of Plenty and one of the most important historic sites in coastal Tauranga. The elegant wooden homestead sits within two hectares of heritage garden that Brown established using plants introduced by early missionaries, many of which survive today. The site documents the complex early relationship between missionaries and tangata whenua in the Bay of Plenty, and the library building on the grounds contains one of New Zealand's oldest surviving personal book collections. The garden's mature trees, including a notable Norfolk Island pine, create a contemplative atmosphere unlike the busy tourism strip nearby.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe The Elms as a quiet and genuinely moving step back in time, with the quality of the heritage garden being as compelling as the house itself. Guided tours add significant depth to the experience.

pois.name: Ohinemutu Living Maori Village
pois.short: A living Maori village on the shores of Lake Rotorua where geothermal steam rises through the ground and around the community's homes, marae, and carved church.
pois.commentary: Ohinemutu is a working residential community of Ngati Whakaue, a hapu of Te Arawa, situated right on the lakefront in Rotorua and is not a tourist recreation but a genuinely inhabited village that welcomes respectful visitors to its public areas. The remarkable Tudor-style St Faith's Anglican Church features a famous window image of Christ wearing a korowai cloak, appearing to walk on the surface of Lake Rotorua behind the altar. Steam vents and bubbling pools emerge throughout the village, including underneath and around homes, reflecting the extraordinary geothermal activity beneath this entire volcanic landscape. The Te Papaiouru Marae and Tama-te-Kapua meeting house are among the most significant carved structures in the region.

reviews.summary: Visitors find Ohinemutu one of the most authentic and affecting cultural experiences in New Zealand, where geothermal wonder and living Maori culture combine in a setting unlike anywhere else on earth.

pois.name: Ohiwa Harbour Scenic Reserve
pois.short: One of New Zealand's most pristine and undeveloped tidal harbours, an important shorebird habitat and traditional mahinga kai area east of Whakatane.
pois.commentary: Ohiwa Harbour is a large sheltered tidal estuary at the eastern edge of the Bay of Plenty, recognised for its exceptional ecological values and its significance to local Maori as a traditional food gathering area. The harbour supports large populations of migratory and resident shorebirds including bar-tailed godwits, pied oystercatchers, and royal spoonbills, and the surrounding wetlands provide important nursery habitat for fish and shellfish. Several reserves encircle the harbour offering walking tracks with views across the glassy water to the Ohiwa spit and the bush-covered hills beyond. The harbour has largely escaped the development pressure that has affected much of the Bay of Plenty coast, preserving a remarkable sense of tranquility.

reviews.summary: Ohiwa Harbour is praised by birdwatchers and kayakers as one of the Bay of Plenty's most rewarding and untrammelled natural experiences. The lack of crowds and the harbour's mirror-calm waters on still mornings make it memorable.

pois.name: Rotorua Redwoods Whakarewarewa Forest
pois.short: A forest of giant Californian redwoods on the edge of Rotorua offering world-class mountain biking trails, elevated treewalks, and peaceful forest walking.
pois.commentary: The Whakarewarewa Forest contains a remarkable stand of Californian coastal redwoods planted in 1901 as a forestry trial, whose towering trunks now create a cathedral-like environment just minutes from Rotorua's city centre. The forest has developed into one of New Zealand's premier mountain biking destinations with more than 130 kilometres of trails ranging from beginner-friendly to elite downhill runs, drawing riders from across the country. An elevated Treewalk of 28 bridges suspended between giant redwoods offers a bird's-eye view of the forest floor 12 metres below, illuminated at night by lanterns. The forest also contains walking trails, a bike park, and a visitor hub with equipment hire.

reviews.summary: Mountain bikers from around the world rate Rotorua's Redwoods trails as among the finest in the southern hemisphere, while non-cyclists are equally impressed by the sheer scale and serenity of the giant trees.

pois.name: Otanewainuku Rainforest
pois.short: A remarkable 1200-hectare tract of never-logged indigenous rainforest just 20 minutes from Tauranga, sheltering rare species including kiwi and long-tailed bats.
pois.commentary: Otanewainuku is one of the last large examples of intact lowland tawa forest in the Bay of Plenty and is exceptional for never having been logged or heavily farmed. The forest floor is a lush tangle of ferns, mosses, and fallen logs draped in epiphytes, providing habitat for North Island brown kiwi, long-tailed bats, and a full complement of forest birds including tui, bellbird, and kereru. The main track follows a stream valley for 4 kilometres before climbing through increasingly dense canopy to a ridgeline lookout. The experience of walking in a landscape that has remained essentially unchanged for centuries has a profound quality that few accessible forests in the Bay of Plenty can match.

reviews.summary: Hikers and naturalists treasure Otanewainuku as one of the North Island's most ecologically intact forest experiences within easy reach of a major city. The silence and density of the old-growth canopy are frequently mentioned.

pois.name: McLaren Falls Park Glowworm Walk
pois.short: Free outdoor glowworm display along a bush-lined stream after dark.
pois.commentary: McLaren Falls Park south of Tauranga hosts one of New Zealand's best free glowworm experiences. After dark, a short walk along the park's stream trail reveals thousands of glowworms lighting up the bush-lined banks like a galaxy of tiny blue-green stars. Unlike commercial glowworm caves, this is an open-air experience where you walk freely along the stream with a torch. The effect is most dramatic on warm, damp, overcast evenings. By day, the park is a popular picnic and walking destination with a waterfall and lake.

reviews.summary: Magical free experience that rivals commercial caves. Best on warm damp evenings. Turn off your torch and let your eyes adjust.

pois.name: Moutohora / Whale Island
pois.short: Predator-free volcanic island wildlife sanctuary with rare native species.
pois.commentary: Moutohora (Whale Island) rises from the Bay of Plenty about nine kilometres off the Whakatane coast, a volcanic island that has been transformed into a predator-free wildlife sanctuary. The island supports populations of rare species including North Island saddleback (tieke), red-crowned kakariki, and the ancient reptile tuatara, all reintroduced after predator eradication. Access is limited to guided DOC-approved tours that run during summer.

reviews.summary: A rare privilege to visit an actively recovering sanctuary island. Seeing saddleback and tuatara in the wild is extraordinary.

pois.name: Motu Trails / Opotiki
pois.short: Nga Haerenga Great Ride linking coast, river gorge, and bush by bike.
pois.commentary: The Motu Trails are part of New Zealand's Nga Haerenga Great Rides cycle trail network, comprising three linked trails around Opotiki at the eastern end of the Bay of Plenty. The Dunes Trail follows the coast through sand dune country, the Motu Road section climbs through dramatic river gorge scenery, and the Pakihi Track traverses regenerating native bush. Together they offer over 100 kilometres of riding from easy coastal paths to challenging backcountry routes.

reviews.summary: Excellent trail network through diverse landscapes. The Motu River gorge is stunning. Well-maintained trails with good facilities.

pois.name: Whakaari / White Island Volcano Cruise
pois.short: Scenic boat tour to NZ's most active marine volcano in the Bay of Plenty.
pois.commentary: Whakaari / White Island is New Zealand's most active marine volcano, lying 49 kilometres off the Whakatane coast. Following the tragic 2019 eruption, landing tours are no longer operating, but scenic boat cruises circle the island offering views of the steaming crater and dramatic cliff faces. The two-hour boat trip from Whakatane crosses open water and often encounters dolphins, whales, and seabirds en route. On-board guides explain the island's geological and human history.

reviews.summary: A powerful experience seeing an active volcano up close from the water. The 2019 eruption context adds gravity.

pois.name: Mauao / Mount Maunganui Summit
pois.short: Iconic coastal summit walk with panoramic views over the Bay of Plenty.
pois.commentary: Mauao (Mount Maunganui) is the iconic 232-metre volcanic cone guarding the entrance to Tauranga Harbour, and the summit walk is the Bay of Plenty's most popular short hike. The well-formed track climbs steadily through pohutukawa-clad slopes to a panoramic summit with views extending from White Island to the Coromandel Peninsula. The base track offers an easier alternative, circling the mountain along the coast. The adjacent Main Beach is consistently rated among New Zealand's best.

reviews.summary: The perfect short hike with incredible views and a beach reward at the bottom. Sunrise from the summit is magical.

pois.name: Te Puia / New Zealand Maori Arts & Crafts Institute
pois.short: Maori arts institute with carving and weaving schools beside Pohutu Geyser.
pois.commentary: Te Puia in Rotorua is home to the New Zealand Maori Arts & Crafts Institute, where master carvers and weavers train the next generation of traditional Maori artists, and the Whakarewarewa Thermal Valley, featuring the famous Pohutu Geyser that erupts up to 30 metres high. The guided tours combine cultural experiences with walks through the dramatic geothermal landscape. Te Puia operates as a social enterprise, with revenue supporting the preservation and transmission of traditional Maori arts.

reviews.summary: The best combination of Maori culture and geothermal activity in NZ. The carving school is extraordinary.

pois.name: Kiwifruit Country / Te Puke
pois.short: Interactive kiwifruit attraction in the world's kiwifruit capital.
pois.commentary: Te Puke proudly calls itself the Kiwifruit Capital of the World, and Kiwifruit Country offers an interactive experience exploring New Zealand's signature fruit. The attraction includes orchard tours on a kiwi kart, tastings of fresh and processed kiwifruit products, and displays explaining the history and cultivation of the fruit — originally Chinese gooseberries, rebranded and commercialised in New Zealand from the 1960s. The gift shop sells kiwifruit wines, jams, and skincare products. Te Puke's surrounding orchards are a vivid green patchwork in summer and the town itself has a relaxed rural character. The Big Kiwifruit slice outside town is an essential photo stop for fans of New Zealand's Big Things.

reviews.summary: Fun and educational, especially for families. The kiwifruit tasting is surprisingly varied. Quick stop on the way through Te Puke.

pois.name: Katikati Mural Town
pois.short: Self-proclaimed Open Air Art Gallery with 70+ heritage murals across town.
pois.commentary: Katikati has reinvented itself as New Zealand's Mural Town, with over 70 large-scale painted murals adorning buildings throughout the town centre, each depicting scenes from the district's history — from Maori settlement through European immigration to modern agriculture. A free self-guided mural walk map is available from the information centre and the full circuit takes about an hour. The murals are complemented by the Haiku Pathway along the Uretara Stream, where stepping stones and boulders are inscribed with haiku poems, and the Western Bay Heritage Museum. The town's location between Tauranga and Waihi makes it a natural stopping point, and several good cafes serve travellers.

reviews.summary: More murals than you'd expect in a small town. The heritage themes are interesting and the haiku pathway adds a creative touch.

pois.name: Karangahake Gorge
pois.short: Historic gold-mining gorge with tunnels, bridges, and bush walks along the Ohinemuri River.
pois.commentary: Karangahake Gorge between Paeroa and Waihi is one of the North Island's finest walking destinations, combining dramatic river gorge scenery with fascinating gold-mining heritage. The Windows Walk follows a path carved through tunnel windows in the cliff face above the Ohinemuri River, offering dramatic views down into the gorge. The rail trail follows the old railway line through tunnels alongside the river. Mining relics including stamper battery foundations, water races, and tunnel entrances dot the valley. The Crown Mine tunnel walk uses a torch-lit passage through a 1-kilometre mining tunnel. The gorge is particularly beautiful in autumn when the surrounding bush turns golden. Multiple walk options range from 30 minutes to full-day circuits.

reviews.summary: Outstanding combination of scenery and history. The Windows Walk is dramatic. The mining tunnel walk is atmospheric.

pois.name: Ohope Beach
pois.short: 11km golden sand beach consistently rated among NZ's best.
pois.commentary: Ohope Beach stretches 11 kilometres of golden sand along the eastern Bay of Plenty, consistently rated one of New Zealand's best beaches. The beach faces northeast, creating warm, sheltered swimming conditions in summer, while the western end near Ohope Spit catches good surf. The beachside village has a relaxed, uncommercialised feel with a handful of cafes and accommodation options. The Nga Tapuwae o Toi walkway connects Ohope to Whakatane via a clifftop track with spectacular coastal views. The Ohiwa Harbour at the eastern end is one of the most biodiverse estuaries in New Zealand, supporting shellfish beds and wading birds. The beach is popular with families and retirees who appreciate its safe swimming and gentle pace.

reviews.summary: A gorgeous, underrated beach with safe swimming. Less crowded than Mount Maunganui. Ohiwa Harbour adds another dimension.

pois.name: Comvita Visitor Centre
pois.short: World-leading manuka honey company with tastings and bee education.
pois.commentary: Comvita's visitor centre in Paengaroa showcases the story of New Zealand manuka honey, from the native manuka bush to the finished product. Interactive displays explain the unique antibacterial properties that have made UMF manuka honey a globally sought-after health product. Honey tastings allow visitors to compare different UMF grades and floral varieties. The centre also covers bee ecology and the threats facing bee populations worldwide. A well-stocked shop sells the full Comvita range at competitive prices. The Bay of Plenty's warm climate supports prolific manuka flowering, making this an appropriate setting for the story. The visit takes about an hour and is free.

reviews.summary: Educational and interesting, even if you're not a honey enthusiast. The tastings are generous. Good prices in the shop.

pois.name: Whakatāne Heads & Pōhaturoa Rock
pois.short: Sacred rock and walkway telling the powerful story of Wairaka and the Mātaatua waka.
pois.commentary: Whakatāne Heads is one of the most historically significant sites in the Bay of Plenty. Pōhaturoa Rock, in the town centre, is a sacred Māori site associated with the arrival of the Mātaatua waka (canoe) from Hawaiki. The story of Wairaka — a woman who broke tradition by taking the paddle to save the waka when it drifted — is central to the town's identity. The Ngā Tapuwae o Toi walkway climbs from the town to the headland, passing significant cultural sites with interpretive panels. The views from the pa site at the top encompass White Island and the Bay of Plenty coastline. This is a walk rich in cultural meaning rather than just scenic beauty.

reviews.summary: Rich in cultural history and stunning views. The Wairaka story is powerful. The headland walk is well interpreted.

pois.name: Whakaari / White Island Scenic Flights
pois.short: Scenic flights over NZ's most active volcano — a steaming island 50km offshore.
pois.commentary: Whakaari / White Island is New Zealand's most active marine volcano, lying 50km off the Bay of Plenty coast. Following the tragic 2019 eruption that killed 22 people, landing tours have been suspended, but scenic flights and boat tours offer dramatic views of the steaming, sulphur-yellow island from a safe distance. Helicopter and fixed-wing flights circle the island, providing aerial views of the active crater, acidic lake, and the remnants of the old sulphur-mining operation. The island's violent recent history adds a sobering dimension to its geological drama. The flights also provide spectacular views of the Bay of Plenty coastline and the volcanic arc extending to the Kermadec Islands.

reviews.summary: Dramatic aerial views of an active volcano. The recent eruption history adds gravity. Scenic flights are the way to see it now.

pois.name: Papamoa Hills Cultural Heritage Regional Park
pois.short: Walk through ancient pā earthworks with panoramic Bay of Plenty views.
pois.commentary: Papamoa Hills Cultural Heritage Regional Park protects several significant pre-European Māori pā sites on coastal hills above the Bay of Plenty. Walking tracks climb through the well-preserved earthworks — terracing, defensive ditches, and kumara storage pits are clearly visible in the landscape. The summit views are spectacular — spanning the Bay of Plenty coastline from Mount Maunganui to Whakatāne, with the volcanic cone of Whakaari/White Island visible offshore. The park is managed by Bay of Plenty Regional Council, with interpretive panels explaining the pā's defensive design and the people who lived there. The combination of cultural significance, archaeological interest, and panoramic views makes this one of the region's most rewarding short walks.

reviews.summary: Fascinating pā earthworks with outstanding coastal views. Well interpreted. A meaningful walk with cultural depth.

pois.name: Ōhiwa Harbour Kayak
pois.short: Kayak one of NZ's most pristine harbours — rich birdlife, shellfish beds, and mangrove channels.
pois.commentary: Ōhiwa Harbour is one of New Zealand's most ecologically pristine tidal harbours, and exploring it by kayak reveals its extraordinary natural wealth. The harbour's sheltered channels, mangrove-lined margins, and vast intertidal flats support diverse birdlife — oystercatchers, godwits, dotterels, and shags are abundant. The water clarity allows views of shellfish beds and marine life below. Kayak tours and hire operate from several access points around the harbour. The harbour's relatively undeveloped shoreline — bush, farmland, and a few scattered settlements — preserves a sense of wildness unusual for a harbour so close to urban areas. Guided tours provide ecological context and birding expertise.

reviews.summary: A pristine harbour best explored by kayak. Birdlife is exceptional. Water clarity is remarkable. Peaceful and uncrowded.

pois.name: Minden Lookout
pois.short: Panoramic viewpoint over the entire Bay of Plenty — harbour, islands, and Mount Maunganui.
pois.commentary: The Minden Lookout is one of the Bay of Plenty's best viewpoints — a roadside lookout on the Minden Road between Te Puke and the coast, with sweeping panoramic views over Tauranga Harbour, Mount Maunganui, Matakana Island, and across the Bay of Plenty to Whakaari/White Island. On clear days, the view extends along the coast in both directions. The lookout is free, easy to access by car, and provides an excellent orientation to the region's geography. The best times are early morning (for soft light) and late afternoon (for golden-hour photography). There are no facilities — just a parking area and the view — which keeps it refreshingly simple.

reviews.summary: Outstanding panoramic views for zero effort. The bay, harbour, and islands spread below. Best in morning or late afternoon light.

pois.name: Moutohora (Whale Island) Wildlife Refuge
pois.short: Predator-free island sanctuary with rare native species off Whakatāne.
pois.commentary: Moutohora (Whale Island) is a predator-free wildlife refuge in the Bay of Plenty, accessible only by guided tour from Whakatāne during the summer months (December-March). The volcanic island supports thriving populations of native species including North Island saddleback, Cook's petrel, and a resident tuatara population. The island's geothermal activity is visible as steaming vents along the shoreline. Guided day trips include a boat journey, island walking, wildlife interpretation, and often excellent whale and dolphin spotting en route. The island has significant Māori history, with pā sites and traditional fishing grounds. Limited tour numbers protect the fragile ecosystem. A rare opportunity to experience a predator-free island ecosystem in the making.

reviews.summary: Visitors love the rare wildlife encounters and the sense of visiting a protected sanctuary. The boat trip often includes dolphin sightings. Book well ahead.

pois.name: Mauao Base Track / Mt Maunganui
pois.short: Iconic coastal walk around and over Mauao with stunning beach views.
pois.commentary: Mauao (Mount Maunganui) is one of New Zealand's most iconic natural landmarks — a 232-metre volcanic cone at the tip of the Mount Maunganui beach peninsula. The base track is a 3.4km loop around the mountain, offering stunning views of the ocean beach, Tauranga Harbour, and Matakana Island. For those wanting more, the summit track climbs to the top in about 30-40 minutes, rewarding with 360-degree panoramic views of the Bay of Plenty coastline and, on clear days, White Island steaming on the horizon. The area holds great significance to local iwi as a ancestral pā site. The mountain rises directly from the main beach, making it easy to combine a walk with swimming and café visits along the beach front. Free access at all hours.

reviews.summary: One of NZ's most satisfying short walks. The summit views are incredible and the beach is right there for a post-walk swim. A Bay of Plenty essential.

pois.name: Aotearoa Breweries
pois.short: Innovative brewery making beers with native NZ ingredients like hāngi flavours.
pois.commentary: Aotearoa Breweries in Kawerau pushes the boundaries of New Zealand craft beer by incorporating native ingredients and cultural flavours into their brewing. Their standout range includes the Taniwha, a beer with smoky hāngi flavours, and Frisky Feijoa, a fruit beer celebrating one of NZ's most beloved fruits. The brewery also produces beers using mānuka honey, kawakawa, and other native botanicals. The taproom offers tastings and the chance to learn about the inspiration behind each beer. Located in the eastern Bay of Plenty, the brewery is a refreshing stop for travellers between Rotorua and the East Cape. The innovative approach to blending Māori cultural elements with craft brewing makes it a unique food and drink experience.

reviews.summary: Beer enthusiasts love the innovative native ingredient beers. The Taniwha and Frisky Feijoa are standouts. A unique NZ craft beer experience.

pois.name: Whakaari / White Island Viewpoint (Whakatāne Heads)
pois.short: Clifftop viewpoint overlooking Whakaari/White Island volcano.
pois.commentary: The Whakatāne Heads lookout provides a powerful vantage point from which to observe Whakaari/White Island, New Zealand's most active marine volcano, steaming on the horizon 49km offshore. Since the tragic December 2019 eruption that claimed 22 lives, the island has been closed to visitors, and this coastal viewpoint has become a place of reflection and remembrance. On clear days, the volcano's steam plume is clearly visible. The headland itself offers a beautiful coastal walk with views along the Bay of Plenty coastline. A memorial to the 2019 eruption victims is located in Whakatāne town. The area holds deep significance for local Ngāti Awa iwi. The viewpoint is freely accessible and provides context for understanding the volcanic forces that shape the Bay of Plenty region.

reviews.summary: A sobering and thought-provoking viewpoint. Seeing the volcano steaming offshore puts the 2019 tragedy in perspective. The coastal walk is beautiful.

pois.name: McLaren Falls Park Glow Worm Walk
pois.short: A free evening walk through native bush to see thousands of glow-worms sparkling along a stream bank — a magical alternative to Waitomo.
pois.commentary: McLaren Falls Park between Tauranga and Taupō offers one of New Zealand's most accessible free glow-worm experiences. After dark, a short walk along the stream in the park reveals thousands of glow-worm larvae (Arachnocampa luminosa) lighting up the bank like a constellation brought to earth. The effect is genuinely magical — blue-green pinpricks of light twinkling in the darkness. Bring a torch for the walk in but switch it off at the viewing area to see the full display. No booking required, no entry fee. The park also has a lake, waterfall and excellent daytime walking. A Tauranga after-dark must-do.

reviews.summary: A magical free experience. The glow-worms are abundant and beautiful. Bring a torch and wear sturdy shoes. Best on dark, overcast nights. Can be busy in summer but the stream bank is long enough for all.

pois.name: Tūhua / Mayor Island Marine Reserve
pois.short: A volcanic island marine reserve in the Bay of Plenty with exceptional diving, snorkelling, and rare obsidian beaches.
pois.commentary: Tūhua (Mayor Island) is a dormant volcano rising from the Bay of Plenty, surrounded by a marine reserve that protects some of the best subtropical diving in New Zealand. The crystal-clear waters host large schools of fish including kingfish, trevally and pink maomao, along with spectacular kelp gardens and reef formations. The island itself features rare obsidian (volcanic glass) beaches and a crater lake. Access is by charter boat from Tauranga — typically a day trip with time for diving or snorkelling and a walk ashore. The island is a DOC nature reserve and landing permits may be required during certain seasons.

reviews.summary: Outstanding marine life and diving. The obsidian beaches are unique. The boat trip is part of the adventure. Weather can cancel trips — be flexible. A genuine Bay of Plenty highlight.

pois.name: Whakaari / White Island Volcano
pois.short: New Zealand's most active marine volcano — a smouldering island visible from the Bay of Plenty coast, now viewed by scenic flight.
pois.commentary: Whakaari (White Island) is New Zealand's most continuously active volcano, sitting 49 km offshore in the Bay of Plenty. The island is a dramatic sight — steaming, stained with sulphur, and ringed by turquoise sea. Following the tragic 2019 eruption that killed 22 people, island landings have been suspended, but scenic flights and boat tours circle the volcano, offering dramatic aerial views of the active crater, steaming vents and sulphur-yellow deposits. The island remains a powerful reminder of New Zealand's position on the Pacific Ring of Fire. Views from the mainland coast at Whakatāne and Ōhope are possible on clear days.

reviews.summary: A dramatic sight even from a distance. Scenic flights offer excellent views. Landing tours are currently suspended. The 2019 eruption adds a sombre dimension. Check weather for best viewing conditions.

pois.name: Whakatāne Heads & Pōhatu Pā Walk
pois.short: Walk to a dramatic headland pā site above Whakatāne — where the Mataatua waka first landed, with sweeping views of the Bay of Plenty.
pois.commentary: The Whakatāne Heads walk climbs to one of the most dramatically positioned pā sites in the Bay of Plenty. The headland was the landing place of the Mataatua waka, one of the great migration canoes, and remains deeply significant to local iwi. The walk passes through coastal bush and along cliff tops to viewpoints overlooking the river mouth, the town, White Island, and the sweep of the Bay of Plenty. Interpretation panels tell the story of the Mataatua landing and the establishment of the settlement. The combination of dramatic scenery, cultural significance and accessible walking makes this one of the best short walks in the eastern Bay.

reviews.summary: A rewarding coastal walk with great views and important cultural history. The pā site is dramatic. Interpretation panels are well-done. Some steep sections. Best in good weather for the views.

pois.name: Karangahake Gorge — Windows Walk
pois.short: Heritage walk through mining tunnels with dramatic gorge window views.
pois.commentary: The Windows Walk follows the old rail line through the Karangahake Gorge, passing through tunnels blasted for the gold-era railway. Windows cut into the tunnel walls frame spectacular views of the gorge below — a unique heritage walk combining industrial history with dramatic scenery.

reviews.summary: Brilliant heritage walk — the tunnel windows are spectacular.

pois.name: Ōmanawa Falls
pois.short: Hidden waterfall cascading into a bush-fringed pool near Tauranga.
pois.commentary: Ōmanawa Falls is a beautiful 12-metre waterfall tumbling into a deep pool surrounded by native bush. After years of closure for safety upgrades, the newly opened track provides safe access to this previously forbidden gem — one of the Bay of Plenty's most scenic waterfalls.

reviews.summary: Worth the wait — the new track makes this stunning waterfall accessible.

pois.name: Maketū Estuary Walk & Pies
pois.short: Coastal estuary walk in the home of New Zealand's most famous meat pies.
pois.commentary: Maketū is famous for two things: the landing place of the Te Arawa canoe and the legendary Maketū Pies. Combine a visit to the pie shop with a walk around the Maketū Estuary, a significant wetland for migratory wading birds and an important site in Māori history.

reviews.summary: Great pies and a lovely estuary walk — a perfect NZ combination.

pois.name: Whakaari / White Island Viewing — Whakatāne
pois.short: Coastal viewpoint for New Zealand's most active marine volcano.
pois.commentary: Whakaari / White Island is visible from the Whakatāne waterfront, a steaming cone rising from the Bay of Plenty. While landing on the island is restricted following the 2019 eruption, the Whakatāne memorial and viewing points allow reflection on both the volcano's power and the lives lost.

reviews.summary: The volcano is visible from shore — the memorial is deeply moving.

pois.name: Athenree Homestead
pois.short: A restored kauri homestead tied to Katikati's planned Irish settlement, with period rooms and gardens.
pois.commentary: Athenree Homestead is a compact, human-scale way to read the Bay of Plenty's settler history. The kauri house dates from the late 1870s and is linked with the planned Ulster settlement around Katikati, when families arrived to turn paper promises of land and prosperity into real farms, gardens and houses.
